Cooking carrots actually increases the bioavailability of beta-carotene.
Carrots are 88% water.
The carrot is the second most popular vegetable in the world after the potato.
Baby carrots are simply large carrots cut and shaped by machine, not a separate variety.

interesting_facts: Spinach originated in Persia (modern-day Iran) over 2,000 years ago.
Popeye's association with spinach led to a 33% increase in US spinach consumption in the 1930s.
Spinach contains oxalic acid which can bind to iron and calcium, reducing their absorption.
One cup of raw spinach contains only 7 calories.
Spinach has more protein per calorie than most other vegetables.

Sulforaphane in broccoli activates the body's own antioxidant defences.
Broccoli has more protein per calorie than steak.
One cup of broccoli has nearly as much Vitamin C as an orange.
Broccoli belongs to the same species as cabbage, kale, and Brussels sprouts.

interesting_facts: Botanically a fruit, the tomato is legally classified as a vegetable in the United States since an 1893 Supreme Court ruling.
Cooking tomatoes increases lycopene content by up to 35%.
China is the world's largest producer of tomatoes.
There are over 10,000 varieties of tomatoes.
Tomatoes were once thought to be poisonous in Europe because they belong to the nightshade family.

interesting_facts: The potato originated in the Andes of South America and has been cultivated for over 8,000 years.
Potatoes were the first vegetable to be grown in space (1995, aboard the Space Shuttle Columbia).
There are over 4,000 varieties of potato worldwide.
The Irish Potato Famine (1845–1849) caused the death or emigration of over 2 million people.
Cooling cooked potatoes increases resistant starch content by up to 75%.

interesting_facts: Onions cause tears because cutting them releases syn-propanethial-S-oxide, which reacts with eye moisture to form sulphuric acid.
Onions are among the oldest cultivated vegetables, with evidence of cultivation dating back 5,000 years.
China produces more than half of the world's onions.
Ancient Egyptians considered the onion a symbol of eternal life due to its concentric rings.
Red onions contain significantly more quercetin and anthocyanins than white or yellow varieties.

interesting_facts: Allicin, the key compound in garlic, is only formed when garlic is crushed or chopped — not when it is whole.
Garlic has been used medicinally for over 5,000 years across Egyptian, Greek, Roman, Chinese, and Indian cultures.
During World War I and II, garlic was used as an antiseptic to prevent gangrene.
A fear of garlic is called alliumphobia.
China accounts for roughly 80% of the world's garlic production.

Green, yellow, orange, and red bell peppers are all the same plant at different stages of ripeness.
Bell peppers contain no capsaicin (the compound that makes chillies hot).
Red bell peppers have 11 times more beta-carotene than green ones.
Bell peppers are native to Central and South America and were brought to Europe by Christopher Columbus.

interesting_facts: The white colour of cauliflower is maintained by tying the outer leaves to shield the curd from sunlight.
Cauliflower can be found in purple, orange, green (Romanesco), and yellow varieties.
The edible part of the cauliflower is called the 'curd'.
Cauliflower rice has become a popular low-carbohydrate alternative to white rice.
Mark Twain called cauliflower 'a cabbage with a college education'.

interesting_facts: Cucumbers are technically a fruit botanically, but are used and classified as vegetables culinarily.
They are one of the oldest cultivated crops, with evidence of cultivation dating back 3,000 years in India.
Cucumbers are 95% water, making them one of the most hydrating foods.
The phrase 'cool as a cucumber' refers to the fact that the inside of a cucumber is up to 20°F cooler than the outside air.
Cucumbers contain a compound called cucurbitacin, which gives them their slightly bitter taste.

Frozen peas retain more nutrients than fresh peas that have been stored for several days.
Peas fix atmospheric nitrogen through root bacteria, improving soil fertility.
Pea protein is increasingly used as a plant-based protein source in food products.

Sauerkraut (fermented cabbage) was used by sailors in the 18th century to prevent scurvy.
Red cabbage turns blue when cooked due to pH changes; adding vinegar maintains its red colour.
Cabbage leaves have been used historically as poultices for inflammation and joint pain.
China is the largest producer of cabbage globally.

interesting_facts: Despite the name, sweet potatoes are not closely related to regular potatoes.
One medium sweet potato provides over 100% of the daily recommended intake of Vitamin A.
Sweet potatoes are one of the oldest vegetables known to humans, with evidence of cultivation dating back 10,000 years.
The Okinawan purple sweet potato is associated with the longevity of the Okinawan people of Japan.
Sweet potatoes are actually a root vegetable, not a tuber like regular potatoes.

interesting_facts: Beetroot juice has been shown to improve athletic endurance and lower blood pressure within hours of consumption.
The red pigment in beetroot (betacyanin) can temporarily turn urine and faeces red or pink — a harmless condition called beeturia.
During the 19th century, beetroot juice was used as a hair dye.
Sugar beet, a variety of Beta vulgaris, accounts for about 20% of the world's sugar production.
Roman physicians prescribed beetroot as a treatment for fever and constipation.

interesting_facts: Zucchini flowers are edible and considered a delicacy in Italian cuisine, often stuffed and fried.
Zucchini is technically a fruit botanically, like tomatoes and cucumbers.
The world record zucchini weighed over 29 kg (64 lbs).
Zucchini is one of the easiest vegetables to grow at home and is highly prolific in summer.
The name 'zucchini' is Italian, while 'courgette' is used in British English and French.

interesting_facts: During World War II, the UK government encouraged citizens to grow kale because it was easy to cultivate and highly nutritious.
Kale was the most popular green vegetable in Europe throughout the Middle Ages.
A single cup of cooked kale provides over 1,000% of the daily recommended intake of Vitamin K.
Kale belongs to the same species as cabbage, broccoli, Brussels sprouts, and cauliflower.
Kale gained modern 'superfood' status around 2012 and its popularity increased by over 400% in the US.

interesting_facts: The myth that celery has 'negative calories' (burns more to digest than it contains) is false, though it is very low in calories.
Ancient Greeks used celery as a seasoning; ancient Romans used it medicinally.
Celery seeds are used as a spice and in herbal medicine for joint pain and blood pressure.
A single stalk of celery is about 95% water.
Pascal celery, the most common variety, was first cultivated in the late 19th century.

Asparagus takes 3 years to grow from seed before the first harvest.
White asparagus is grown underground or under cover to prevent photosynthesis and chlorophyll production.
Asparagus was considered a luxury food in ancient Rome and Egypt.
Asparagus can grow up to 10 cm per day in ideal conditions.

interesting_facts: Eggplant gets its name from certain white varieties that resemble hen's eggs.
It is native to the Indian subcontinent and has been cultivated for over 4,000 years.
Eggplant absorbs large amounts of oil during frying due to its sponge-like structure.
It belongs to the nightshade family along with tomatoes, peppers, and potatoes.
The purple pigment nasunin is unique to eggplants and is a powerful antioxidant.

interesting_facts: Radishes were used as currency in ancient Egypt, alongside gold and garlic.
The word 'radish' comes from the Latin 'radix', simply meaning 'root'.
Daikon radish (white radish) is one of the most widely consumed vegetables in Asia.
Radishes can be grown from seed to harvest in as little as 3–4 weeks.
Ancient Egyptian labourers who built the pyramids were reportedly fed radishes, onions, and garlic.
